ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Cham Cham specifically; closest defensible anchor is the chenna-based confection class. URI used is approximate; do not treat as exact match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sugar confectionery — milk-based sweet (chenna/paneer base, boiled in sugar syrup)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Cham Cham. A0FJP (sugar confectionery) is the closest defensible category anchor. Annotated as approximate.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Indian sweet preparation.

preparation_method: boiling chenna (fresh cottage cheese) oblongs in concentrated sugar syrup
optional layering with malai (cream) or khoya (milk solids)
optional filling with sweet cream mixture

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Cham Cham is one piece weighing approximately 50 g, providing around 108 kcal, 2.9 g protein, 3.75 g fat, and 16 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods does not list Cham Cham as a standalone entry. Values are adapted from the NIN data for chenna-based sweets (Rasgulla / Sandesh class) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cottage cheese (ID 1015352) and sucrose to account for the sugar-syrup absorption characteristic of Cham Cham. Saturated fat estimated from dairy fat composition of chenna. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available in the adapted source combination and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cham Cham in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) using the closest analogous food: Rasgulla (chenna boiled in sugar syrup, same base preparation), which is referenced in peer-reviewed literature at GI ~65. The input record cited GI 68 (high) from NIN; however, NIN is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. The University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate of 65 is used instead, classified as medium. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 32.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 50 g serving) / 100 / 100 = 10.4 g GL per 50 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (40 mg per 100 g) with beneficial calcium from chenna
hypertension.guidance: Cham Cham is low in sodium and unlikely to raise blood pressure; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total calorie and saturated fat intake, particularly when the sweet is coated with malai or khoya, which increases dairy fat content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined sugar content (28.5 g sugar per 100 g) and medium glycemic index (GI 65) causing postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cham Cham is high in added refined sugar and carries a medium GI of approximately 65, making it unsuitable for routine consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, portion size should be limited to half a piece (~25 g) and paired with a protein or fibre source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from chenna and dairy toppings, combined with high sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from chenna (estimated 4.6 g per 100 g) and the high sugar content are both relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Cham Cham to occasional consumption and prefer the plain version without khoya or malai coating to reduce saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar may promote systemic inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Refined sugar in Cham Cham may exacerbate inflammatory markers in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; consumption should be minimised and replaced with lower-sugar dairy alternatives where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cham Cham (also spelled Chomchom or Chom Chom; Bengali: চমচম; Hindi: चम चम; Odia: ଚମଚମ) is a traditional Bengali milk-based sweet originating in East India, particularly West Bengal. It is prepared by curdling full-fat milk to produce fresh chenna (cottage cheese curd), which is kneaded until smooth and shaped into oblong or cylindrical pieces approximately 5–7 cm in length. These pieces are then simmered in a concentrated sugar syrup until they absorb the syrup and develop a dense, fudgy, slightly spongy texture that distinguishes Cham Cham from the lighter, more porous Rasgulla, which shares the same base. After cooking, Cham Cham is typically rolled in desiccated coconut, layered with malai (clotted cream) or khoya (reduced milk solids), or filled with a sweetened cream mixture. It is often tinted yellow with saffron or pink with food colouring and flavoured with cardamom. Per 100 g, Cham Cham provides approximately 215 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 7.5 g fat (of which ~4.6 g is saturated), and 32 g carbohydrate with 28.5 g sugar. It is a good source of calcium (180 mg) and riboflavin (0.18 mg) from its dairy base. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), making it a moderate-to-high glycaemic food. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar load, and should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally low in sodium (40 mg per 100 g) and gluten-free. Cham Cham is a staple of Bengali celebrations including Durga Puja and Diwali and is commonly offered as a gift sweet.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Cham Cham?
Is Cham Cham good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Rasgulla and Cham Cham?
What is Bengali sweet made from cottage cheese?
meal_context: festive sweet during Durga Puja and Diwali
traditional Bengali dessert after lunch or dinner
gift sweet for celebrations and weddings

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN does not publish a standalone Cham Cham entry; values are adapted from chenna-based sweet analogues (Rasgulla / Sandesh class) in NIN and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cottage cheese data. Saturated fat is estimated from dairy fat composition. GI is an analogous estimate from Rasgulla data via the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for Cham Cham are not available as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (4.6 g) is estimated from the known dairy fat composition of chenna (approximately 60–65% of total fat as saturated fatty acids) and is not directly reported in the adapted source. 3. GI value of 68 cited in the input record was sourced from NIN, which is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2; the value has been revised to 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food (Rasgulla) referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. 4. Portions in the input record contained four identical duplicate entries (all '1 serving 100g'); deduplicated to a single 50 g piece serving as the canonical portion. 5. No zero-macro justification is required as protein_g (5.8), fat_g (7.5), and carbohydrate_g (32.0) are all non-zero.
